steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Data Interpretation question asking which graph data supports the researchers’ conclusion. The hypothesis is that leaders who reflect on lessons learned from past mistakes show more humility than those who don’t. To support it, the graph must show the “learned from mistake” group scoring higher in humility than both the “no learning” group and the control group.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘None of the three groups’ average humility scores exceeded 3.5.’: States a ceiling fact about score range but does not distinguish among the three groups, so it cannot support a hypothesis about which group is more humble. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘The managers in the control group exhibited only slightly less humility on average than the managers in the two experimental groups did.’: Misreads the graph — the control group actually scored higher than the “no learning” experimental group, so treating both experimental groups as a single block is inaccurate and does not support the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘The managers who reflected on a past mistake that resulted in learning exhibited more humility on average than the managers in the other two groups did.’: Directly matches the graph (learning group ~3.1 vs. control ~2.9 and no-learning group ~2.7) and restates the hypothesis in data terms. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘All three groups exhibited less humility on average than the researchers expected.’: The graph provides no information about the researchers’ expectations, so this claim is unsupported. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
